methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Climate & Energy (found 2 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 21 same-sector peers exist in Climate & Energy, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

private.name: QuantumScape
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Public), post-money $5B+

private.name: LanzaTech
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Public), post-money $800M+

data.narrative:
Enapter is a public-stage Climate & Energy company founded by Sebastian-Justus Schmidt. The valuation analysis draws from a comp universe of nine peers: one public company (LanzaTech, ticker LNZA) and eight private firms including QuantumScape, TAE Technologies, Redwood Materials, and Helion. This peer set spans adjacent subsectors within climate and energy infrastructure.

The peer-median method proved inapplicable to this valuation. While 21 same-sector peers exist in Climate & Energy, the narrow filtering criteria—same stage, adjacent stage, or similar valuation tier—yielded fewer than three datapoints in each category. Applying an all-sector median would risk conflating Enapter's specific risk and growth profile with broader climate-tech heterogeneity, introducing material distortion. Consequently, traditional multiples triangulation cannot be reliably executed on the available peer data.

This analysis highlights a structural constraint common in frontier-tech valuation: public comparables in specialized subsectors are sparse, and private peer data often lacks sufficient granularity and consistency for robust statistical triangulation. A credible valuation of Enapter would require either (1) expansion of the comp set via transaction precedents or equity raises, (2) application of DCF or scenario-based methods leveraging operational and market projections, or (3) explicit reliance on bilateral negotiations or investor signaling in recent rounds. The current peer universe is insufficient to support a quantitative multiples-based range.
